title: Te Whakatau Tohu: Navigating the Mangakōtukutuku Stream / Mathematical Navigation of the Mangakōtukutuku Waterway
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will explore mathematical concepts through the lens of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a significant waterway to Ngāti Kahungunu. They will learn how ancestors used mathematical reasoning for navigation, resource management, and understanding seasonal patterns while connecting to the story of Kahungunu and Rongomaiwahine, whose descendants have maintained this knowledge along this waterway for generations.
learningObjectives: Calculate and compare flow rates of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream using measurement and rate calculations
Create scale maps of the stream's path using coordinate geometry and proportional reasoning
Analyze seasonal water level data patterns using statistical measures and graphing techniques
Apply traditional wayfinding concepts to solve modern navigation problems using angles and bearings

activities.title: Te Awa Tūpuna - Ancestral Waterway Mapping
activities.description: Students visit the Mangakōtukutuku Stream to measure distances and create scale maps using coordinate systems, incorporating traditional place names and significant sites along the waterway

activities.title: Whakatau Tohu Taurangi - Calculating Flow Rates
activities.description: Using timed float measurements and cross-sectional area calculations, students determine the stream's flow rate at different points, connecting to how ancestors monitored water movement for travel and resource management

activities.title: Tātari Rautau - Seasonal Pattern Analysis
activities.description: Students analyze historical water level data from local monitoring stations, creating graphs and calculating statistical measures to identify seasonal patterns and extreme weather events

activities.title: Te Arataki - Traditional Wayfinding Application
activities.description: Using angles and bearings, students solve navigation problems along the stream, learning how ancestors used star paths, land markers, and water currents for travel between settlements

activities.title: Whakawhiti Kōrero - Knowledge Sharing Circle
activities.description: Students present their mathematical findings to local kaumātua, explaining how modern mathematics connects to traditional knowledge of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Hawke's Bay Regional Council stream monitoring data and maps
Traditional navigation tools (replicas) and modern measuring equipment
'Ngā Kōrero o Te Awa' - local historical records of the waterway from Hastings District Library

tasks: Create a detailed mathematical report analyzing the stream's flow patterns and navigation challenges
Develop and present a scale map with coordinate references of a section of the waterway
Solve a series of wayfinding problems using angle and bearing calculations
Explain mathematical connections to traditional knowledge in a reflective journal

nzcAlignment: Geometry and Measurement: Use scales, maps, and bearings
Number and Algebra: Calculate rates and ratios, patterns and relationships
Te Tiriti o Waitangi: Honoring Māori mathematical knowledge and traditions
